mnemonics.title: Email Threat Types
mnemonics.content: P-S-W-B-S-M: Phishing → Spear Phishing → Whaling → BEC → Spoofing → Malware (Remember as 'Please Send Weird Bills Soon Mr.')

mnemonics.title: Email Authentication Trio
mnemonics.content: SPF Checks SENDER, DKIM Verifies SIGNATURE, DMARC Enforces POLICY - Think 'SPD for Email Defense'

mnemonics.title: Phishing vs Spear vs Whale
mnemonics.content: Phishing = Mass cast (net), Spear = Targeted (harpoon), Whale = Executive (big catch) - Size increases, specificity increases

acronyms.usage: DNS-based authentication specifying authorized mail servers for a domain
Cryptographic email authentication using public-key signatures

acronyms.expansion: Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance
acronyms.usage: Unified email authentication policy combining SPF and DKIM with enforcement
Email encryption and digital signatures using X.509 certificates
Fraudulent scheme where attackers impersonate executives to steal money/data
Attack where attacker intercepts communications between two parties
End-to-end email encryption using public-key cryptography

visualTips.visualization: Picture three checkpoints: SPF at the gate (who can enter), DKIM with a stamp (verified content), DMARC as the security guard (enforces rules)
Think of a fishing net: Phishing = wide net (many targets), Spear = baited hook (specific target), Whaling = harpoon (executives only)
Layer 1: TLS (transport tunnel), Layer 2: S/MIME/PGP (lock on message) - like a courier in an armored truck delivering a locked safe

analogies.analogy: Like receiving a letter with a forged return address - it looks official but is actually from someone else
Like a bouncer at a club with a guest list (SPF), ID verification (DKIM), and the authority to reject or quarantine (policy)
Like a fisherman casting a baited hook hoping someone bites - the bait looks legitimate but hides danger
Like a bomb disposal expert checking a suspicious package in a blast-proof room before opening it in the office
